title: SEGMENT 7 — Human iPSCs and the cancer problem with c-Myc

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Human iPSCs and the cancer problem with c-Myc

A year and a half after the mouse paper, in November 2007, two papers appeared back-to-back, one in Cell and one in Science, reporting the successful reprogramming of human fibroblasts to pluripotency.
The Cell paper was from Yamanaka's group again.
The Science paper was from a completely independent group — Junying Yu and James Thomson at the University of Wisconsin.
Thomson, by the way, was the researcher who had first derived human embryonic stem cells in 1998, almost a decade earlier.
He was, in some sense, the dean of the field, and he had been quietly working on his own reprogramming approach in parallel with Yamanaka.
The two groups arrived at the same destination at almost the same moment, by different routes.
Yamanaka's human iPSC paper used the same four factors he had used in mouse — OSKM.
He took skin fibroblasts from a 36-year-old woman, introduced the four factors via retroviruses, and after about a month obtained colonies that looked, behaved, and tested like human ES cells.

By every available assay — gene expression, surface markers, embryoid body formation, teratoma formation in immunocompromised mice — these cells were human pluripotent stem cells, derived from an adult skin biopsy.
No embryos involved.
No ethical impasse.
Thomson's paper used a different cocktail.
Instead of OSKM, Thomson's team used Oct4, Sox2, Nanog, and Lin28.
The first two are the same.

The second two are different — Nanog is another pluripotency transcription factor (the same one Yamanaka had used as a reporter for full reprogramming), and Lin28 is an RNA-binding protein that affects which mRNAs get translated in the cell.
The fact that two completely different sets of four factors could both reprogram human fibroblasts told the field something important: there isn't just one recipe.
There's a network of pluripotency-supporting factors, and you can get into that network from multiple entry points.
As long as you turn on enough of the right master regulators, the network self-assembles and pulls the cell up into the pluripotent state.
Now, the human iPSC papers had immediate practical impact, but they also had a problem.
The problem was c-Myc.
Remember from the cancer lecture: c-Myc is one of the most potent oncogenes in human biology.
It's overexpressed in a huge number of cancers — Burkitt lymphoma, neuroblastoma, many breast and colon cancers.
Forcing c-Myc into a cell is, biologically, a dangerous thing to do.
And Yamanaka's reprogramming method did exactly that.
The retrovirus integrated the c-Myc gene into the cell's chromosomes, where it stayed permanently, even after reprogramming was complete.
The iPSC, in its final state, was a cell that had been forced to carry a viral integration of a powerful oncogene.
This was not a hypothetical concern.

When Yamanaka generated chimeric mice from his early iPSCs — by injecting them into embryos and letting them contribute to all the tissues of the developing mouse — about 20% of those mice developed tumors.
The tumors traced back to reactivation of the c-Myc transgene in iPSC-derived tissue.
Reprogramming was working, but it was also seeding cancer.
This was, obviously, a problem for any clinical aspiration.

If you wanted to make patient-specific iPSCs and then differentiate them into, say, dopamine neurons and inject them into a Parkinson's patient's brain, you absolutely did not want those cells to carry an integrated oncogene that might reactivate at any point and turn the implanted tissue into a tumor.
The field responded in two ways.
First, people showed that you could actually leave c-Myc out and still get reprogramming.
It just took longer and the efficiency dropped.
Three factors — Oct4, Sox2, Klf4 — could do it, slowly and inefficiently.
Better than nothing.
Second, and more important, the entire delivery mechanism — the retroviral integration of the factors — had to be replaced.
If the four genes were never integrated into the iPSC's chromosomes in the first place, then there would be no permanent oncogene risk.
The cells could be made truly safe.
That sent the field into a multi-year sprint to develop integration-free reprogramming.
Several different approaches emerged, and they're worth a segment of their own.
